moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with a move that's deceptively flexible—the King's Indian Attack is about controlling the center with pieces rather than occupying it immediately with pawns. Nf3 develops our knight to a powerful square, controls e5 and d4, and keeps all our options open since we haven't committed to a pawn structure yet. This move order lets us adapt to Black's setup while maintaining strategic unity.

Black mirrors our approach with good reason—the knight on f6 controls key central squares (e4 and d5), supports potential pawn breaks, and develops with tempo. Black is following sound opening principles by matching our piece development while keeping flexibility about their own plan.

This is the signature move of the King's Indian Attack, and we're committing to the Spassky Variation's specific setup. By playing g3, we prepare to fianchetto our bishop on g2, which will dominate the long diagonal (a1-h8) and exert tremendous pressure on d5 and e4. This quiet move contains real venom—we're building a solid kingside structure while preparing a slow, grinding game where our piece coordination matters more than tactics.

Black strikes immediately on the queenside, trying to create immediate counterplay before we fully establish our setup. This aggressive move gains space and threatens to push further with b4, attacking our knight and forcing it to make a decision. Black is testing whether we're really ready for a race, so we need to respond with our plan intact rather than getting drawn into defending passively.
